sco_text: Count with meaning to 20 by building quantities; and matching quantity with numeral.
Orally sequence numbers to 100 by 1s, 2s, 5s and 10s.
Count backwards from  20 by 1s, 2s, 5s and 10s.
Count backwards from 100 by 5s and 10s.
Identify error/s in a counting sequence up to 20.
Use Number lines (vertical and horizontal) and number charts to support counting and skip counting.
Counts by 2s, and 5 to find a total in multiplicative contexts (repetitive addition).
Quantify sets up to 20 and state that the last number said is the amount counted.
Identify numeral for quantities and vice versa.
Represent a given number up to 20 using a variety of concrete models, including ten- frames and created materials.
Model a given number up to 20 using a variety of pictorial representation.
Find examples of a given number in the environment.
Place given numerals on a number line (vertical and horizontal) with benchmarks 0, 5, 10, 15 and 20.
Partition any given quantity up to 20 into two parts and identify the number of objects in each part.
Model a given number using two different objects.
Subitize for quantities 1-10.
Build sets and set models to show how numbers compare
Explain which set has more and which set has fewer.
Build a set that has fewer or more than a given set up to 20
Build a set that is between two given sets.
Use ordinal language (first, second, third……)
Discuss the importance of the number 10.
Explain that teens numbers are represented as 10 and more.
Use models such as ten frames to show numbers up to 20 as 10 and some more.
Count teen numbers using familiar numbers.
Model teen numbers up to 20 in a variety of ways using sets of tens and ones.
Recognize that in a teen number, the 1 represents one group of ten.
Explain that teen numbers are composed of one ten and one, two, three, four, five, six, seven, eight, or nine ones.
Compose and decompose numbers from 11 to 19 into tens and ones.
Combine quantities (sets, lengths, etc.) using concrete materials to get the total or find a missing value up to 20.
Partition and recombine quantities to relate to addition and subtraction.
Explain how addition and subtraction are related.
Add and subtract within 20, using a variety of strategies.
Determine if equations involving addition and subtraction are true.
Separate quantities to find a missing value from a number up to 20.
Comparing two quantities to find the difference from a number up to 20
Using symbols for addition (+) and subtraction (-) as a way to record calculations done through stories, building, and drawing.
Make reasonable estimation when combining, separating, comparing & partitioning quantities to 20.
Model and describe equal collections of objects as ___ groups of ___.
Form equal groups of objects and use repeated addition to represent the group.
Skip count to find the total number of objects in groups.
Write mathematical statements to represent groups of equal objects.
Share objects into equal groups and state how many objects are in each group.
Write mathematical statements to represent equal groups of objects shared.
Describe, copy and extend a repeating pattern with 2 to 4 elements (using manipulatives, diagrams, body movements/actions/sounds)
Translate a repeating pattern from one representation or modality to another (E.g., a repeating orientation pattern to a repeating sound pattern, etc.)
Spot and correct errors in a repeating pattern.
Identify and describe repeating patterns in the environment (both man-made and nature)
Show appreciation for their peers’ creation of patterns and patterns in our environment by identifying and describing the pattern observed. around them
Identify and enjoy patterns in poems, stories, (folk and modern) within their culture.
Interpret, describe and extend a given increasing and decreasing pattern that grows by 1 or 2 each time with no more than 4 elements in the core.
Use a variety of tools and models to create, generate and interpret patterns that increase or decrease by 1 or 2 each time (blocks, pattern strips, pattern blocks, etc.)
Identify missing elements and/or errors in a given increasing and decreasing pattern.
Show an appreciation for patterns found in the environment around them by recognizing, talking about, describing and recreating their own examples based on observations.
Identify and describe missing quantities in numbers up to 20 by using a variety of concrete materials to represent situations.

represent missing quantities in numbers up to 20 by writing number sentences in semantic and computational forms: - using joining, separating, part - part whole and comparing structures/ relationships ,  - using the inverse of the given operation
Use different number combinations and linear models to create equivalent number values and lengths up to a total of 20.
Identify and use equivalent relationships of equal length, number and number sentences adding up to 20.
Compare lengths, number, and number sentences using the equal sign.
Determine whether given pairs of expressions are equivalence or balance using concrete objects, as well as operations on whole numbers.
Interpret the meaning of the equal sign correctly and determine if equations involving addition and subtraction are true or false.
Determine the unknown whole number in an addition or subtraction equation with three whole numbers.
Formulate number sentences to solve word problems (including real-life scenarios/situations) involving addition of two or three whole numbers and subtraction of two whole numbers.
Express equalities as different representations of the same quantity (0 to 20).
Use addition and subtraction up to 20 to solve one step word problems involving adding to, taking from, putting together and taking apart, with unknowns in all positions.
Use language that describes 3D objects, such as cube, cone, cylinder, sphere, orally and in writing to describe a picture or object in realworld contexts.
Use language that describes polygons, such as square, rectangle, and triangle, orally and in writing to describe a picture or object in real-world contexts.
Recognize and name 3D shapes.
Recognize and name 2D shapes.
Identify 2D shapes in real world context.
Identify 3D shapes in real world context.
Identify and name the 2D and 3D shapes whenever they undergo transformation.
Recognize and name the basic 2D shapes (circle, square, rectangle, and triangle) and 3D objects (cube, cone, sphere, and cylinder).
Collect, group and analyze data.
Represent and construct the basic 2D shapes and 3D objects using appropriate materials and tools.
Justify the properties of the basic 2D shapes and 3D objects, such as the number of sides, corners, and faces.
Replicate and construct composite 2D shapes by combining the basic shapes in different ways.
Replicate and construct 3D objects by combining the basic 3D objects in different ways.
Compare the characteristics of shapes and objects, such as the number of sides, corners, and faces, and identify similarities and differences between them.
Sort and pattern shapes and objects based on their characteristics, such as the number of sides, corners, and faces, and create patterns using different shapes and colors.
Represent and construct the basic 2D shapes and 3D objects using appropriate materials and tools.
Replicate and construct 3D objects by combining the basic 3D objects in different ways.
Discuss the importance of geometrical shapes in the environment.
Students will volunteer to role play scenarios involving geometry shapes to show their  appreciation for Geometry.
Identify and name 2D shapes and 3D objects, including cubes, cones, cylinders, spheres, squares, rectangles, triangles, and circles.
Describe the attributes of 2D shapes, including the number of sides and corners, and whether the sides are straight or curved.
Describe the attributes of 3D objects, including the number of corners, whether they have flat or curved sides, and whether they are uniform in thickness or come to a point.
Compare and contrast the attributes of different 3D objects, such as noting that a sphere has no corners or flat sides, while a cube has six square faces and eight corners.
Create and extend patterns using 2D shapes, including squares, rectangles, triangles, and circles.
Sort and classify 2D shapes and 3D objects based on their attributes, such as sorting by the number of sides or corners.

Use appropriate vocabulary to describe 2D shapes and 3D objects, such as using the terms "corner" and "vertex" interchangeably or describing a cylinder as having a curved surface and two circular bases.
Identify and name 2D shapes: Students should be able to recognize and name basic 2D shapes such as squares, rectangles, circles, triangles, and hexagons.
Identify and name 3D objects: Students should be able to recognize and name basic 3D objects such as cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres.
Apply knowledge of 2D and 3D shapes in practical contexts, such as solving problems involving shape and space, with support appropriate to their individual needs.
Sort 2D shapes based on their attributes, such as the number of sides, the length of sides, and the number of angles.
Sort 3D objects based on their attributes, such as the number of faces, edges, and vertices.

Compare and contrast 2D shapes and 3D objects identifying similarities and differences between 2D shapes and 3D objects, such as the number of dimensions and the presence of faces, edges, and vertices.
Create 2D shapes and 3D objects using various materials, such as paper, clay, and blocks.
Use mathematical vocabulary related to shapes, such as vertices, edges, faces, and symmetry.
Identify and describe realworld objects that are 2D shapes or 3D objects, such as circles on a clock face or a cone-shaped ice cream cone.
Solve basic geometric problems involving 2D shapes and 3D objects, such as counting the number of faces on a cube or sorting 2D shapes based on their attributes.
Identify the basic shapes, such as circle, square, rectangle, and triangle.
Create and explore with different materials such as playdough, blocks, foam to form different shapes.
Find shapes in the environment such as in buildings, signs, or everyday objects.
Sort shapes based on their attributes.
Identify and create patterns using geometric shapes.
Construct composite objects using different shapes
Identify the different shapes used in the composite objects created.
State the characteristics of the shapes that make them easy or difficult to build with.
Discuss and describe the composite objects.
Compare and contrast different composite objects.
Identify and name at least three different 2D shapes (e.g., circles, squares, rectangles, triangles) that can be found in cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres.
Create and name 2D shapes by using physical objects (such as cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres) to create "footprints" or "shadows" on paper.
Describe the resulting cross-section of slicing a cube, cone, cylinder, or sphere is sliced, the resulting cross-sections can be different 2D shapes, and be able to identify and name these shapes.

Use appropriate vocabulary (e.g., edges, vertices, sides, circles, triangles) to describe the features of cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres, and explain how these features can be used to identify the 2D shapes within them.

Apply knowledge of 2D shapes to real-world objects by identifying and naming the 2D shapes that can be found in everyday objects that resemble cubes, cones, cylinders, and spheres, such as a soccer ball (sphere) or a building block (cube).
Identify and name common 3D shapes, such as cubes, spheres, cones, and cylinders.
Use basic spatial concepts, such as up, down, in front of, behind, and beside correctly.
Use concrete materials, such as playdough, sand, or clay, to create 3D shapes and objects.
Transform 3D objects by molding, shaping, stretching, and flattening the materials.
Identify similarities and differences between transformed and original objects.
Use mathematical language to describe the transformations, such as "flattened," "stretched," or "twisted."
Explore different ways to transform objects, such as rotating, flipping, or sliding.
Use trial and error to solve problems related to transforming objects.
Work collaboratively with peers to create and transform 3D objects.
Reflect on learning and identify areas for improvement in their ability to transform 3D objects.
Explore physical materials and describe them using the vocabulary associated with linear measure (short/long, high/low, near/far, thick/thin, narrow/wide, deep/shallow).
Classify the measuring attribute of length based on the vocabulary associated with it.
Explore physical materials and describe them using the vocabulary associated with capacity (full, empty, same as).
Categorise the measuring attribute of capacity based on the vocabulary associated with it.
Explore physical materials and describe them using the vocabulary associated with mass of an object (heavy/light).
Categorise the measuring attribute of mass based on the vocabulary associated with it.
Explore physical materials and describe them using the vocabulary associated with area of a surface of an object (large/small).
Categorise the measuring attribute of area based on the vocabulary associated with it.

Compare the length of two objects using direct comparison (i.e: place side by side and align one end) and explain reasoning using appropriate vocabulary; (shorter/longer, higher/lower, deeper/shallower, nearer/farther, thinner/ thicker, wider and narrower).
Compare the mass of two objects (lifting, pushing,  pulling and balancing) and explain reasoning using appropriate vocabulary;
Compare the capacity of two objects (filling and emptying) and explain reasoning using appropriate vocabulary (such as more or less, level is higher than or less than).   .
Compare the area of two objects using direct comparison(i.e which one occupies more space) and explain reasoning using appropriate vocabulary (such as bigger and smaller).
Measure length, mass and capacity using non standard units
Select appropriate non standard units to measure different attributes.
Compare the capacity of two containers to determine which holds more.
Use parts of their body to conduct nonstandard measurement. (Palms, hand span, foot span)
Use objects to conduct non-standard measurement. (little stones, blocks, sand, toy cars, paper clips etc.)
Estimate and measure the mass and length of objects using non-standard measurement.
Apply the correct non-standard measurement to measure various items. Example: You would not use a hand span to measure capacity.
Show enthusiasm using non-standard measurement to measure objects.
List examples when they estimate measurement in their everyday lives.
Appreciate the importance of using estimations in everyday situations.
State the days of the week, months of the year and holidays.
Explain the composition of days for week, weeks for month and months for year.
Identify the months in which various holidays occur.
Identify 5c, 10c and 25c and relate their value to pennies (1c)
Read the date on the calendar.
Interpret details on a calendar.
Represent and recognize time on the hour and half past the hour on the analog and digital clock.
State their birthdate with pride.
Be willing to share the date every morning in class.
Construct simple questions based on observation.
State the information that can be collected by using a given question.
Identify sources for collecting information for data.
Formulate questions that can be addressed with data collected to answer them
Observe objects/ people in the environment.
Communicate with others and among peers regarding their observations.
Count the number of objects/ people based on observation.
Justify reasons for answers
Make connections with numbers
Willingly participate in observation activities.
Learn to appreciate that questions that are worded differently can obtain the same answer.
Accept that information can come from multiple sources.
Sort objects/ people according to one attribute (shape, colour, size, properties, ethnicity, occupation, etc.)
Represent the information collected from observations /experiments/ interviews in multiple ways.
Use a variety of different methods (counting, matching, grouping, rearranging, ordering, using counters, etc.) to collect and sort data.
Collect data in different ways to answer questions.
Use symbols/tables/groupings to sort and organise data collected.
Appreciate the fact that different information can be derived from objects sorted.
Show willingness to collect, organise and display data
Explain how and why a given data set can be arranged in different orders.
State the highest and least value in each data set.
Compare the values of different members of a given data set
Arrange sets of data from greatest to least
Use a variety of strategies (tally charts, matching, counters, array diagrams, bar charts, etc.) to identify the count of each member of given data set.
State reason(s) why identified members in a data set has a low or high frequency.
Identify and speak about trends in data set presented in tables, pictographs and or concrete bar charts.
Represent given data on a tally or graph.
Arrange data according to frequency.
Order sets of data displayed from greatest to least using tallies, concrete graph and pictographs.
Appreciate the fact that the same data can be represented differently.
Speak freely of how different strategies used by students helps in making learning interesting.
Identify that data can be represented in tally charts, tables, concrete graphs, and pictographs
Read and interpret data represented in tables, pictographs and bar charts
Distinguish among the different ways in which data can be represented
Read and interpret data presented in various ways.
Generate and answer questions based on data presented in tally charts, concrete graphs, pictographs
Use correctly vocabulary (most, least, more than, less than, etc.) when speaking about the occurrences of members in a data set
Draw conclusions based on the analysis of information given.
Analyze data to obtain essential information.
Interpret information from data presented in graphs, tally charts, tables, concrete charts.
Appreciate the importance of being able to present sets of data  in multiple ways
Begin to accept that different students can interpret the same data in different ways.
Students make informed decisions based on the information received.
Use correctly words (vocabulary) associated with probability such as “impossible” ‘will happen”, (will never happen’, “possible”, and “certain to describe the likelihood of events occurring
Predict possible outcomes of events given.
Make informed decisions based on predictions and outcomes of event.
Appreciate the importance of learning about probability and how the knowledge of such can be applied to our daily lives and affect decisions we make.
Listen and speak for pleasure and personal growth
Choose to listen to music, poetry and stories for pleasure.
Connect environmental sounds to meaning.
Use different voices in role playing to indicate tone and mood.
Describe how musical and environmental sounds affect mood.
Use Listening Comprehension Strategies to make and simplify meaning:  ask questions, recall ideas, predict, visualise, and make connections.
Listen to, retell, and express an opinion about the story.
Listen to differentiate between make-believe and truth.
Listen to follow and give direction e.g. 2-3 steps.
Listen and respond with increasing understanding  and confidence to conversations expressed in Standard English
Listen and speak to form and foster relationships
Use turn taking strategies as a listener and a speaker.
Identify and use some non-verbal cues (e.g., facial expressions, gestures etc.) and in oral communication with thoughtfulness.
Listen, share information ask and and answer questions on a topic opinion in response to views articulated by others.
Listen and speak to develop appreciation and celebration of culture and oral languages.

Continue to develop Home Language and Standard English vocabulary, phrasing and sentence use to share ideas.

Use common singular and plural nouns in the meaningful context of sentences(e.g. She runs, We run)

reflect on and connect interests and background knowledge before, during and after reading.

connect background knowledge to new learning.
regard reading/viewing as sources of interest, enjoyment, and information

participate in opportunities to share favourite texts with peers

select, with teacher assistance, texts appropriate to their interests and reading progress
select just right texts with assistance and beginning independence
use illustrations in a text to describe and interpret characters, setting, and events

formulate questions as well as understandings of digital fiction and nonfiction text,

Retell the beginning, middle, and end of stories and events using images, drama, models and written summaries, using
Use illustrations, graphics etc. to ask and answer questions, draw conclusions and make inferences, identify details, synthesise main main idea from the details

Continue to make connections between Home Language vocabulary and Standard English vocabulary to the language of the story, poem, song, or visual representations,
Apply known oral vocabulary within familiar language patterns to make meaning from text,
Continue to develop use of context clues to solve unknown vocabulary,
Begin to automatically read and understand sight and some highfrequency words and words of personal interests or significance in a variety of reading contexts
Demonstrate understanding (not definitions) and application of synonyms, antonyms, and homophones

Expand vocabulary by noticing and using frequently occurring root words to read inflectional forms (e.g., look, looks, looked, looking)
Develop visualisation strategies to connect words, and phrases of Home Language and Standard English text to meaningful mental images
Demonstrate understanding of how word order in fiction and nonfiction text impacts meaning,
Begin to apply punctuation(full stop, question mark, exclamation mark) and bold print to guide intonation and expression, change the tone, and for emphasis, etc.
Apply automatic knowledge of known sight words within sentences  to develop phrasing, fluency, and expression

Apply knowledge of word order, illustrations, text content and initial, final and some medial letter sounds to identify unknown words,

Why? -does the author use unusual words? - What’s an example?
Engage with and participate in Shared Reading and Readers Theatre in fiction, nonfiction, and poetry to connect reading with personal and audience engagement.
Develop an understanding of text features such as bold print and font size adds to an author’s style.

Continue to develop understanding and purpose of fiction, nonfiction, and poetry genres as represented by Emergent and Early Stage: Pictures ,Poems ,Stories ,Fantasy stories, Informational texts
Apply some text features to locate and predict information in text: title, table of contents, illustrations, labels on diagrams, back blurb, numbered steps
Read and follow simple  written and/or illustrated instructions
Use text features to support individual and/or small group research on topic of interest
Search for answers to questions on digital sites
Discuss interesting words that evoke emotions
Create a personal word bank
Develop an understanding that words may have similar meanings
Continue to develop an understanding of how written word order impacts meaning.
Compare texts by same illustrator for similarities in mood (happy, funny, exciting, etc.)
Explore how emoticons, symbols, and shapes are used to convey or enhance meaning
Begin to develop strategies for the prewriting stages of the writing process
Talk about the ideas they plan to write about; provide learners with prompts that are of interest and relevant
Draft some illustrations to accompany ideas and write thoughts that match the ideas in their drawings
Use role play to assist with brainstorming ideas
Begin to use simple graphic organizers to brainstorm and plan
Begin to become familiar with the purpose of various genres and how different genres look and sound: narratives,  poetry, expository
Begin to develop strategies for the connecting prewriting to the drafting stages of the writing process
Write to match text to their brainstorm, planning and drawings
Begin to identify the topic, purpose, audience and form for writing
Begin to experiment with creating a draft in different forms e.g. poems, invitations, excuses, informal letters
Begin to experiment with writing in simple sentences that may include descriptive words or simple transition words (and, but, then etc.).

With assistance, begin to become familiar with the keyboard for individual or shared writing
Work with a partner, in small groups and independently, to create a draft writing in both print and/or digital format
Continue to use role plays to assist with drafting process planning, convey and enhance meaning
Apply lessons learned about writing to record feelings, thoughts and ideas during Independent or Journal writing time.
engage in teacher assisted and peer collaboration to review the organisation of the written draft through the process of ARMS: Add, Remove, Move, Substitute
revisit brainstorming and illustration and illustration to add details to and/or delete details.
Reread their draft and begin to recognize where they can make changes to better organise the beginning, middle, and end; first, second third, etc.
Experiment with developing writing with a sense of flow
Begin to establish a personal voice in their writing by using pictures and words that convey their attitude or feelings towards the subject or audience
Begin to extend writing to include vocabulary learned during reading and conversation
Experiment with using multisensory words (hearing, smell), taste, etc.) to improve writing
Begin to use comparison words to distinguish one thing from another—size, shape, texture
Become comfortable making decisions about when to include Home Language and/ or Standard English
Monitor and revise writing to ensure simple but complete sentences are meaningful
Develop more understanding of how and when to use descriptive or action words to extend meaning of draft
apply knowledge of spoken language to know when to use pronouns, plurals, and words indicating position (above, below, under, etc.)
experiment with some variety in first sentences (leads) of writing
demonstrate understanding of the connection between word order and meaningful writing
Begin to use various types of sentences: declarative, question, and exclamation
Use techniques demonstrated by favorite authors (descriptive words, funny words, short sentences, long sentence, asking questions, etc.)
Use expressive language to elaborate and create interest
Develop increased confidence, and accuracy willingness to try out various strategies (risk taking) to increase spelling and punctuation accuracy, tidiness  and presentation
Write from left to right and from top to bottom consistently
Use some conventional spacing
Continue to develop a relaxed hand while writing
Attempt to make letter formation readable by various audiences
Monitor and check high frequency words with the aid of: environmental print, word walls, personal dictionaries

Generate temporary and accurate spelling based on phonological strategies such as: -using words posted on the word wall and/or in an individual word book. - stretching out sounds or use sound boxes.
-developing awareness of sequencing and blending sounds (beginning, middle and ending of a word)
-clapping syllables and spelling each syllable separately.
-thinking about spelling patterns from known or rhyming words.

Continue to develop spelling of single syllable words by applying knowledge of: -beginning and ending consonants
-two-letter initial consonant blends - beginning and some ending consonant digraphs
-short and long vowel sounds
-knowledge of vowel clusters

-CVCC (consonant, vowel, consonant, consonant)
-CVVC (consonant, vowel, vowel, consonant)
-CVCE (consonant, vowel, consonant, e)
Apply letter and word knowledge  strategies during the writing process to monitor (notice errors), edit errors and check for correctness.
Apply sight word knowledge gathered through reading and writing to “try out” different spellings of a word to see which one looks right.
Begin to be aware of digital features such as spell check
Spell commonly used sight words and phonetically regular words with increasing correctness in final copies

Begin to add a few grade-level punctuation conventions: -full stops
-question marks
-exclamation marks
-capitals on names and at the beginning of a sentence.

Develop hand created or digital presentation styles using various forms of hand or digitally generated: -illustrations
-titles
-fonts
Participate in opportunities to share (through in person or digital format) a chosen piece of writing with peers or an invited audience

•	waves
•	loud
•	Soft

Practice new vocabulary in discussions.

Appreciation: Students will develop an appreciation for the importance of water as a precious natural resource and recognize the need for conservation efforts.
Interest/Curiosity: Students will develop a deep interest and curiosity in the topic of water by exploring and investigating its properties, distribution, and importance to living things.
Inventiveness: Students will use their creativity and inventiveness to develop and implement solutions to real-world water-related problems, such as water pollution, scarcity, and distribution.

Active Participation: Students will participate actively in classroom discussions related to water, developing critical thinking and communication skills through classroom debates, discussions and presentations about water.
